rescue_reasoning: Keep: The policy text explicitly links the target to climate change and an adaptation outcome. Quotes: 'reduce cases of human vector-borne diseases associated with climate change by 10% by 2030' and 'Reduction in the number of cases of human vector-borne diseases associated with climate change'. This is a direct climate-sensitive health risk reduction indicator consistent with Adaptation. The gate failure appears due to missing/strict outcome-token matching rather than non-climate content.
policy_statement: NAP target: reduce cases of human vector-borne diseases associated with climate change by 10% by 2030 (decadal average).
mechanism_description: Set a target to reduce the number of climate change–associated human vector-borne disease cases through vector-borne disease prevention and control actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (warming/changed precipitation) → ↑ vector suitability/abundance → disease control measures → ↓ human exposure & transmission → ↓ climate-related health impacts
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: Public health and environmental management actions (surveillance, source reduction, vector control, personal protection, rapid diagnosis/treatment) targeted at vectors and transmission settings. (2) Mediating process: Reduced vector populations and/or reduced human–vector contact and interrupted pathogen transmission cycles despite climate-driven increases in vector habitat suitability. (3) Outcome: Fewer cases/outbreaks of vector-borne disease and reduced climate-related health risk (lower morbidity/mortality and health system burden).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced human morbidity/mortality by lowering exposure to climate-amplified vector-borne diseases.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: climate-driven shifts in vector ecology increase disease risk; interventions reduce exposure/transmission, lowering health impacts—an exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway. 2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: the main benefit is reduced harm from climate hazards, not reduced emissions (Mitigation), reduced resource inputs (Resource Efficiency), or ecosystem carbon/heat services as the central lever (Nature-Based Solutions). 3) Edge cases: some measures (e.g., wetland drainage, pesticide use) may have environmental co-effects, but the climate-relevant intent and primary impact remain health risk reduction under climate change. 4) Applies to all instances: any policy with this mechanism—targets/programs to cut climate-associated vector-borne disease cases via prevention/control—operates through reducing exposure and vulnerability, fitting Adaptation. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., public health preparedness, early warning, surveillance) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy achieves risk reduction primarily via ecosystem restoration (e.g., habitat management framed as nature-based), it could also involve Nature-Based Solutions, but under this canonical mechanism the dominant pathway is public health adaptation (surveillance/control) to reduce exposure.
key_indicators: “NAP target”; “reduce cases”; “vector-borne diseases associated with climate change”; “10%”; “by 2030”; “decadal average”
co_benefits: Broader public health improvement and reduced non-climate-related vector-borne disease burden.
instance_edge_case_notes: Target could be interpreted as a performance standard, but it is framed as a national adaptation plan target (outcome goal) rather than an enforceable standard applied to regulated entities.
